name: Orange County, California
tagline: California's 3rd-most-populous county (~3.2M residents): a centralized internal IT shop, OC Information Technology (OCIT), runs technology for county agencies, with a ~$10.5B FY2026-27 budget and an active cloud-modernization push shaping near-term tech spend.
overview: Orange County is the local government for ~3.2 million residents — California's third-most-populous county, behind Los Angeles and San Diego — and one of the largest urban-county technology buyers in the state. IT is centralized: OC Information Technology (OCIT) is the county's internal service provider, delivering data-center, applications, client-services, networking, and security across roughly two dozen county agencies under the County Executive Office. OCIT is best understood as a shared-services shop that bills the operating departments it serves (Social Services, Health Care, Sheriff, Assessor, Registrar of Voters, and others) rather than a standalone agency with a large discretionary budget of its own — the program dollars largely sit with those departments. The county's FY2026-27 Recommended Budget is about $10.5B all-funds ($5.2B general fund), released May 20, 2026, with Board adoption scheduled for June 23, 2026. For a Dell SLG seller, the combination of a centralized IT buying center, a large stable budget, and an in-flight cloud and ERP modernization (Oracle Fusion for finance/HR, selected May 2025) points the motion toward infrastructure refresh, hybrid-cloud and data-center modernization, and security — routed through the county's central procurement office.

budget.intro: Orange County's FY2026-27 Recommended Budget totals about $10.5B all-funds, of which ~$5.2B is the General Fund and ~$1.3B is General Purpose Revenue covering mandated services. It was released May 20, 2026, with Board of Supervisors adoption scheduled for June 23, 2026. IT spend isn't published as a single public line — it flows through OCIT's internal cost-recovery model and the budgets of the ~23 county agencies it serves, so the buying signal is in department modernization plans rather than a standalone IT appropriation.

stats.label: County budget (FY26-27)
stats.note: All-funds Recommended Budget released May 20, 2026; adoption scheduled June 23, 2026

stats.label: General Fund
stats.note: FY26-27; ~$1.3B General Purpose Revenue for mandated services

notes: OCIT is funded largely through internal service charges to the county agencies it supports, not a single large general-revenue IT line.
Program and modernization dollars sit with the operating departments (Social Services, Health Care, Sheriff, Assessor, etc.) — map the funded department initiative, not just OCIT.

initiatives.name: Oracle Fusion Cloud ERP (finance & HR)
initiatives.summary: The county selected Oracle Fusion Cloud Applications in May 2025 to move finance and HR off legacy systems. A multi-year program signaling appetite for cloud modernization and a reference point for the infrastructure, data, and integration work that surrounds a major ERP migration.

initiatives.name: Cloud services migration & data-center strategy
initiatives.summary: OCIT has run cloud-migration solicitations and lists Cloud, Mobility, Open Data, and Infrastructure as its core technology strategies. The pitch is hybrid-cloud and data-center modernization that pairs on-prem refresh with cloud workloads — Dell's ISG and as-a-service consumption fit this track.

initiatives.name: County Cybersecurity Program
initiatives.summary: OCIT runs a formal county Cybersecurity Program — reducing security risk while enabling secure information exchange across county agencies and partners. FY25-26 strategic priorities lead with strengthening system and data security, making security a durable, recurring entry point for endpoint, identity, and infrastructure-security offerings.

initiatives.name: High-availability network & systems refresh
initiatives.summary: OCIT's stated FY25-26 priorities include implementing a high-availability network and enhancing county systems and applications. As a centralized shared-services provider, it carries ongoing server, storage, network, and end-user compute refresh across agencies — a steady lifecycle-and-resilience motion.

procurement.intro: Selling to Orange County runs through its central County Procurement Office (CPO), which issues solicitations and manages vendor registration. Two paths shorten the cycle: national cooperative contracts the county has already adopted, and California statewide leveraged agreements the county can buy off without a fresh RFP.

vehicles.name: County Procurement Office (CPO)
vehicles.detail: The central buying office for county solicitations, terms, and vendor registration (cpo.oc.gov) — the front door for direct county business.

vehicles.name: Cooperative contracts (OMNIA Partners, NASPO ValuePoint)
vehicles.detail: The county leverages national cooperatives — e.g., an OMNIA Partners agreement (#R240303 via Carahsoft) available to agencies within Orange County — to buy off pre-competed awards. Confirm Dell's cooperative coverage and reseller scope.

vehicles.name: California statewide LPAs (CMAS / Cal eProcure)
vehicles.detail: California Multiple Award Schedules and other DGS leveraged procurement agreements let the county buy off pre-competed statewide contracts — verify Dell's CMAS/LPA coverage is active and correctly scoped.

powerStructure.name: Michelle Aguirre
powerStructure.title: County Executive Officer (CEO)
powerStructure.note: Top of the county administration and budget; appointed June 2025, OCIT reports up through the CEO's office. Sets the spending frame every agency operates within.

powerStructure.name: KC Roestenberg
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Officer (OCIT)
powerStructure.note: Owns county IT strategy and the OCIT shared-services organization — the central technology relationship for the account. Confirm current incumbency before forecasting.

powerStructure.name: Ed Althof
powerStructure.title: Assistant CIO & Chief Technology Officer (OCIT)
powerStructure.note: Deputy/CTO who manages the department in the CIO's absence — a key technical and operational relationship in the buying center.

powerStructure.name: Andrew Alipanah
powerStructure.title: Information Security Director & County Privacy Officer (OCIT)
powerStructure.note: Owns the county Cybersecurity Program and privacy posture — the security buying-center relationship for endpoint, identity, and infrastructure-security plays.

powerStructure.name: Board of Supervisors
powerStructure.title: Five-member governing board
powerStructure.note: Adopts the county budget and approves major contracts — the body scheduled to adopt the FY2026-27 budget on June 23, 2026.

news.title: County releases ~$10.5B FY2026-27 Recommended Budget
news.impact: Large, stable spending plan ($5.2B GF) released May 20, 2026, with Board adoption set for June 23 — the funding backdrop and timing anchor for the account.

news.title: Michelle Aguirre appointed County Executive Officer
news.impact: New top of the county administration that OCIT reports up through — rebuild the executive relationship map and confirm tech priorities under the new CEO.

news.title: County selects Oracle Fusion Cloud Applications for finance & HR
news.impact: A major cloud-ERP modernization signals appetite to retire legacy and opens surrounding infrastructure, data, and integration work — a proof point for the next modernization conversation.

news.title: OCIT advances cloud services migration solicitation
news.impact: Active cloud-migration procurement confirms the hybrid-cloud and data-center modernization track — a near-term opening for ISG and as-a-service positioning.

dellActions: Anchor the relationship with the OCIT buying center — CIO KC Roestenberg and Assistant CIO/CTO Ed Althof — and confirm current incumbencies before mapping the org and forecasting.

Lead with hybrid-cloud and data-center modernization tied to the Oracle Fusion ERP migration and OCIT's cloud-services track — position Dell ISG, storage, and as-a-service consumption around the surrounding infrastructure.
Build the security motion with Information Security Director / County Privacy Officer Andrew Alipanah around the county Cybersecurity Program — endpoint, identity, and infrastructure resilience.

Verify Dell's cooperative coverage (OMNIA Partners, NASPO ValuePoint) and California CMAS/LPA scope so the county can buy without a fresh RFP; register and track solicitations through the County Procurement Office (CPO).
Time the motion around the June 23, 2026 budget adoption and OCIT's FY priorities (security, high-availability network, systems enhancement) when funding and roadmaps firm up.
